name: stability in the sense of Dahlquist
statement_text:
A linear multistep method [α, β] is ‘stable’ if the difference
equation (403a) has only bounded solutions.

Because stability concepts of one sort or another abound in the theory of
initial value problems, ‘stability’ is often referred to as ‘zero-stability’ – for
example, in Lambert (1991)) – or as ‘stability in the sense of Dahlquist’.

variables.name: [α, β]
variables.definition: linear multistep method with coefficients α = (α1, α2, ..., αk) and β = (β0, β1, ..., βk)

variables.name: k
variables.definition: number of steps in the linear multistep method

equations.content: yn = α1 yn−1 + α2 yn−2 + · · · + αk yn−k

preamble: The definition concerns a linear multistep method [α, β] applied to an initial value problem. For the specific one-dimensional problem where f(x, y) = 0, the method reduces to the linear difference equation (403a).
introduces: stability in the sense of Dahlquist
